era_context: Late 14th century: The Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China following the collapse of Mongol Yuan rule. In Europe, the Western Schism divides papal authority between Rome and Avignon. The Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans. The Hundred Years' War between England and France continues with shifting fortunes. Sub-Saharan African kingdoms (Mali, Songhai, Great Zimbabwe) maintain trade networks; the Indian subcontinent fragments under competing sultanates.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European chronicle coverage; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Southeast Asia (sparse European sources; local chronicles exist but underrepresented in Western archives)
Oceania and Pacific islands (virtually no contemporary written record)

events.title: Urban VI dies; Clement VII elected as Antipope, deepening Western Schism
events.description: Pope Urban VI died on 15 October 1379. The Roman cardinals, under pressure from the Roman mob and fearing loss of papal authority to Avignon, elected Clement VII (Robert of Geneva) as antipope on 20 September 1378, though some sources date his election to 1379. This formalized the Western Schism, with two competing papal claimants and their respective colleges of cardinals. The schism would persist for nearly 40 years, fracturing Catholic Christendom and weakening papal prestige. Different regions recognized different popes based on political allegiance.
key_figures: Catherine of Siena (mystic and papal advisor)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Western Schism chronology

events.certainty_note: Papal elections are well-documented in contemporary registers and near-contemporary chronicles. The exact date of Clement VII's election is sometimes given as 1378 or 1379 depending on source; modern scholarship generally accepts September 1378 as the formal election, though the schism deepened through 1379.

events.title: Zhhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) consolidates Ming control; purges court officials
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398) continued aggressive consolidation of Ming power in 1379, including purges of officials suspected of disloyalty or corruption. These purges, part of the broader campaign to eliminate Mongol Yuan remnants and centralize imperial authority, resulted in thousands of executions. The emperor's paranoia and authoritarian methods established a pattern of imperial despotism that would characterize much of the Ming Dynasty. By 1379, the Yuan had been effectively eliminated as a territorial power, though Mongol khans continued to rule in the north.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming court records and later official histories document the purges and consolidation campaigns. Exact casualty figures vary by source, but the broad pattern is well-attested.

events.title: Battle of Pločnik: Serbian forces defeat Ottoman army
events.description: On 28 June 1379 (or possibly 1386, sources vary), Serbian forces under Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović defeated an Ottoman army at Pločnik in central Serbia. This was one of the last significant Serbian military victories before Ottoman dominance became entrenched in the region. The victory was temporary; Ottoman expansion continued, and the decisive Battle of Kosovo (1389) would reverse Serbian fortunes. The Pločnik victory is celebrated in Serbian tradition as a moment of resistance, though its long-term strategic impact was limited.

sources.name: Serbian oral tradition and epic poetry

events.certainty_note: The date of the Battle of Pločnik is disputed. Some sources place it in 1379, others in 1386. Contemporary documentation is sparse; most accounts come from later Ottoman and Serbian chronicles. The battle's historical significance is somewhat inflated in Serbian nationalist historiography.

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) campaigns in Persia and Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, continued his campaigns of conquest and consolidation across Central Asia and Persia in 1379. By this year, he had established himself as the dominant military power in the region, defeating rival khans and nomadic confederations. His campaigns were characterized by extreme violence and the destruction of cities that resisted. Timur's empire would eventually stretch from Anatolia to India, though it fragmented after his death in 1405. In 1379, he was consolidating control over Khwarezm and the Oxus valley.
key_figures: Tokhtamysh (Khan of the Golden Horde, rival)

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (Book of Victories)

events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns are documented in later Persian and Islamic chronicles, some written decades after events. Contemporary documentation is limited. The broad outline of his conquests is secure, but specific dates and details of individual campaigns vary by source.

events.title: John of Gaunt leads English expedition to Castile
events.description: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster and uncle of Richard II of England, led a military expedition to Castile in 1379 to support his claim to the Castilian throne (through his wife Constance of Castile). The campaign was part of the broader Hundred Years' War context and Iberian dynastic conflicts. The expedition achieved limited military success and was ultimately abandoned, but it demonstrated English interest in Iberian affairs and the complex web of dynastic claims that characterized late medieval European politics.
key_figures: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster
Henry of Trastámara (King of Castile, opponent)

sources.name: English royal records and letters patent
Historiographic consensus on Hundred Years' War

events.certainty_note: English royal records document the expedition. Froissart provides narrative detail, though his account is sometimes romanticized. The campaign's dates and outcomes are well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Pločnik's date is contested; some modern scholars place it in 1386 rather than 1379, and its strategic significance has been inflated in Serbian nationalist historiography relative to its actual impact on Ottoman expansion. The Western Schism is sometimes portrayed as a sudden rupture in 1378, but recent scholarship emphasizes the gradual breakdown of papal authority and the complex political maneuvering that preceded the formal schism. Timur's campaigns are often presented as purely destructive, but recent work highlights his administrative consolidation and patronage of culture alongside military conquest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1379 is uneven. European events (papal schism, English politics, Iberian conflicts) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are detailed but compiled later and reflect imperial ideology. Ottoman and Balkan events are documented primarily in later chronicles with significant gaps in contemporary sources. Central Asian and Persian events rely heavily on later compilations (Zafarnama, written in the 15th century). Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific events are virtually absent from surviving written records. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles (especially Chinese and Ottoman) may exaggerate the power and achievements of their patrons.

graph_edges.note: Clement VII's election deepens the Western Schism initiated in 1378
Hongwu's consolidation continues the Ming Dynasty's establishment
Serbian victory at Pločnik precedes the decisive Ottoman victory at Kosovo
Timur's campaigns of 1379 contribute to his empire's eventual extent by 1405
John of Gaunt's Castilian campaign is part of broader English-Iberian dynastic politics
